abstract: This paper extends the classic 'hostile media effect' to the domain of misinformation, proposing a 'hostile misinformation effect' whereby citizens believe their political in-group is targeted more by misinformation than their out-group. Using a three-wave panel survey (N=4,045) conducted around the 2024 European Parliament elections in Germany, the Netherlands, and Poland, the authors find robust evidence of this bias. The effect is amplified by political interest, party identity strength, ideological extremity, and right-wing orientation, though, contrary to expectations, electoral winners (not losers) show a somewhat stronger effect.
key_claims: A 'hostile misinformation effect' exists: citizens systematically perceive their preferred party as more targeted by misinformation than their disliked party.
This effect is theoretically grounded in motivated reasoning and social identity theory, and parallels the well-established hostile media effect.
Susceptibility to the effect is moderated by political interest, party identity strength, ideological extremity, and right-wing orientation.
The effect appears robust across three different European political contexts and slightly strengthens over the course of an election campaign.
Contrary to attribution-bias predictions, election losers do not show a stronger hostile misinformation effect; if anything, winners do.
Hostile misinformation perceptions are associated with lower media trust and lower perceived electoral integrity, suggesting downstream democratic consequences.
methods: Three-wave online panel survey administered by Kantar before, during, and after the June 2024 European Parliament elections.
Samples in Germany (W1 n=2,016), the Netherlands (W1 n=1,846), and Poland (W1 n=2,790); analyses focus on W3 (n≈2,945) with W2 robustness checks.
Quota sampling on age, gender, and region; data weighted on age, gender, and education for descriptives.

Key dependent measures: 7-point items asking how much respondents felt their preferred party and least-preferred party were targeted by misinformation; parallel items for left-wing vs right-wing sides.

Moderators: political interest (1-7), propensity-to-vote score as proxy for party identification, ideological extremity (folded 0-10 left-right scale), left-right position, and winner-loser status (operationalized via vote-share change vs prior national election, with EP 2019 and subjective measures as robustness checks).

Analytical strategy: data stacked so each respondent contributes two in-group/out-group evaluations; linear multilevel random-effects regressions with country fixed effects and pre-registered design (OSF).
findings: 49.6% of respondents believe their own party is at least somewhat targeted by misinformation versus only 27.3% for the opposing party.

On a 7-point scale, the mean perceived targeting of one's own party (~4.55) is roughly one full point higher than for the disliked party (~3.51) in W3; differences are highly significant (t≈20, p<.001).
Multilevel regression confirms a strong in-group/out-group gap (b=1.05, p<.001), supporting H1.
Political interest significantly moderates the effect (b=0.28, p<.001); citizens with very low interest show essentially no hostile misinformation effect.
Stronger party identification (via PTV) amplifies the effect (b=0.20, p<.001); weak identifiers (PTV ≤4) do not exhibit it.
Ideological extremity strengthens the effect (b=0.25, p<.001), though more moderately than interest or identity.
Right-wing respondents show a stronger hostile misinformation effect than left-wing respondents (b=0.13, p<.001); the radical right shows the largest interaction (b=1.58).

Winner-loser status is not significant in the main specification (b=0.00, p=.94); alternative operationalizations suggest, if anything, winners (especially radical-right winners) show a stronger effect, contradicting H5.
The effect replicates when using ideological in-group/out-group rather than party (b=0.94, p<.001) and across W2, and holds across all three countries (slightly weaker in the Netherlands).
Exploratory analyses link the hostile misinformation effect to lower media trust (b=-.08, p<.001) and lower perceived electoral integrity (b=-.05, p<.001).
contributions: Introduces and empirically demonstrates the 'hostile misinformation effect' as a conceptual extension of the hostile media effect to the misinformation domain.
Provides one of the first cross-national panel studies of citizens' perceptions of misinformation targets during a real high-stakes electoral campaign.
Identifies key individual-level moderators (political interest, party identity, ideological extremity, right-wing orientation) that shape vulnerability to this perceptual bias.
Offers comparative evidence across three European countries (Germany, Netherlands, Poland) with varying media trust, press freedom, and political histories.

Suggests that high perceived prevalence of misinformation may partly reflect identity-driven perceptual bias rather than actual exposure, helping explain the gap between estimated and actual misinformation exposure.
Provides preliminary evidence linking biased misinformation perceptions to declines in media trust and perceived electoral integrity.
framing: The paper positions itself within political communication scholarship on misinformation perceptions and bias, explicitly building a bridge between the classic hostile media effect literature (Vallone et al. 1985; Hansen & Kim 2011) and contemporary research on misinformation, fake-news perceptions, and information disorders. It draws theoretically on motivated reasoning (Kunda) and social identity theory (Tajfel & Turner; Huddy), arguing that identity-driven assessments of falsehood are central to understanding why citizens feel surrounded by misinformation in polarized, high-choice media ecosystems.
